skill.name: pnpm
skill.description: Guides pnpm package management, workspaces, configuration, dependency overrides, patches, catalogs, CI setup, and migration from npm or Yarn.
skill.summary: Use pnpm workflows for strict Node.js dependency management, monorepos, CI installs, and package configuration.
skill.author: antfu

security_audit.summary: Static analysis found many command, network, filesystem, script, and sensitive-file patterns. Manual review found these are Markdown guidance and examples for pnpm, CI, configuration, hooks, and migration rather than hidden executable skill code. The skill is publishable with a medium warning because following the guidance can install packages, run lifecycle scripts, edit dependency configuration, read .npmrc files, or publish packages.

medium_findings.title: Package Manager Command Guidance Can Mutate Projects
medium_findings.description: The static command findings are true string matches in Markdown documentation, not hidden executable code. They still carry operational risk because the guidance includes install, test, build, patch, publish, and removal commands that can change dependencies, execute lifecycle scripts, delete generated directories, or publish artifacts if run without review.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The files are documentation, so there is no direct automatic execution. Confidence is high that user-approved execution of these commands can materially alter a project.

medium_findings.title: Sensitive npm Configuration Handling Requires Care
medium_findings.description: The skill tells agents to check .npmrc and documents registry token configuration. This is legitimate pnpm guidance, but .npmrc files can contain private registry tokens, so agents should avoid copying token values into prompts, logs, or generated output.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited content explicitly references .npmrc and auth token configuration. It does not exfiltrate secrets, but careless use could expose sensitive values.

medium_findings.title: pnpm Hooks and Lifecycle Script Controls Affect Executed Code
medium_findings.description: The references describe .pnpmfile.cjs hooks, package scripts, and build-script controls. These are normal pnpm features, but they influence JavaScript code that runs during resolution or installation and should be reviewed in untrusted repositories.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The hook and script examples are benign documentation. The risk is contextual because applying them can cause project-controlled JavaScript to run during installs.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URL Findings Are Documentation References
low_findings.description: The network findings are mostly registry examples and source reference URLs in Markdown. They do not show unauthorized requests or data exfiltration by the skill itself.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs appear in explanatory Markdown examples and source comments. No code path sends local data to those URLs.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Matches Lack Semantic Evidence
low_findings.description: The scanner reported weak cryptographic algorithm blockers at many Markdown lines, but manual review found pnpm documentation, dependency names, version pins, and examples rather than cryptographic code. No evidence found of MD5, SHA1, insecure cipher use, or custom crypto implementation in the reviewed files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Representative cited lines contain package-manager documentation, not crypto APIs. The static label appears to be caused by broad keyword matching.

low_findings.title: No Prompt Injection Evidence Found
low_findings.description: Manual review and targeted search did not find instructions that attempted to override the evaluator, claim pre-approval, skip analysis, or impersonate system messages.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The checked skill entry points and references contain ordinary pnpm documentation. Targeted search found no prompt-injection phrases.

dangerous_patterns.title: Documentation Includes High-Impact pnpm Commands
dangerous_patterns.description: Examples include install, recursive run, patch, publish, store prune, and migration cleanup commands. These commands are expected for a pnpm skill but should be confirmed before execution.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands are visible documentation examples. The risk depends on whether an agent executes them in a user repository.

dangerous_patterns.title: Documentation References Token-Bearing Configuration Files
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill points agents to .npmrc and includes an auth token configuration example. This is a valid pnpm use case, but token values must be redacted in any user-facing output.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The token example is explicit and relevant to npm registry configuration. There is no evidence the skill collects or transmits the value.

content.value_statement: Node.js teams need reliable package installs, workspace commands, and dependency controls. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code focused pnpm guidance for common project workflows.
actual_capabilities: Explains pnpm install, add, remove, update, run, exec, dlx, workspace, store, audit, pack, and publish commands.
Guides pnpm-workspace.yaml, .npmrc settings, package.json pnpm fields, and environment-based configuration.
Supports workspace filters, catalogs, overrides, aliases, patches, hooks, and peer dependency rules.
Provides CI, Docker, caching, frozen lockfile, Corepack, and monorepo build patterns.
Helps migrate npm, Yarn, and Lerna projects to pnpm with rollback considerations.
Documents performance options such as store caching, offline installs, script controls, and workspace concurrency.
limitations: The skill is documentation only and does not verify the current pnpm release automatically.
Some commands can modify files, install packages, run lifecycle scripts, or publish packages.
Project-specific registry, token, and workspace settings still need human review before use.
Examples may need updates for teams using newer pnpm versions or custom CI platforms.

use_cases.title: Set up monorepo installs
use_cases.description: Configure pnpm workspaces, shared lockfiles, filters, and catalogs for a multi-package repository.

use_cases.title: Migrate package managers
use_cases.description: Move an npm, Yarn, or Lerna project to pnpm while handling lockfiles, phantom dependencies, and peer dependencies.

use_cases.title: Improve CI reliability
use_cases.description: Add frozen lockfile installs, pnpm caching, Corepack setup, Docker builds, and targeted workspace jobs.

prompt_templates.title: Install dependencies
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the pnpm skill to explain the safest install command for this project. Check workspace and lockfile context before suggesting commands.

prompt_templates.title: Add workspace package
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the pnpm skill to add a dependency to one workspace package. Include the filter command and any package.json changes I should review.

prompt_templates.title: Migrate from npm
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the pnpm skill to plan an npm to pnpm migration. Cover lockfile import, missing dependencies, CI updates, and rollback steps.

prompt_templates.title: Design monorepo CI
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the pnpm skill to design CI for a large pnpm monorepo. Include caching, frozen installs, changed-package filters, and script safety checks.

output_examples.input: How should I install dependencies in CI?
output_examples.output: Use a frozen lockfile install after setting up pnpm with Corepack or pnpm/action-setup. Cache the pnpm store, and review lifecycle script needs before enabling script execution.

output_examples.input: Can I replace a vulnerable transitive dependency?
output_examples.output: Use pnpm overrides when a patched version is compatible. Document the reason, test the dependency graph, and remove the override after upstream fixes it.

output_examples.input: What should I check before migrating from Yarn?
output_examples.output: Review workspace definitions, remove Yarn-only files only after backup, import or regenerate the lockfile, and test peer dependency warnings before rollout.

best_practices: Review pnpm-workspace.yaml, package.json, lockfiles, and .npmrc before running commands that change dependencies.
Use frozen lockfile installs in CI and make lifecycle script execution an explicit decision.
Document overrides, aliases, patches, and hooks so future maintainers understand why they exist.
anti_patterns: Do not run publish, patch, migration, or removal commands without confirming the target package and repository state.
Do not copy registry tokens or private .npmrc contents into prompts, logs, or generated documentation.
Do not suppress peer dependency warnings or use broad overrides without compatibility testing.

faq.question: Does this skill run pnpm commands automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides guidance that an agent may use. Command execution depends on the host tool and user approval settings.

faq.question: Why does the audit mention command execution?
faq.answer: The references contain many pnpm, shell, Docker, and CI command examples. They are documentation, but they can be risky if executed blindly.

faq.question: Can it help with pnpm workspaces?
faq.answer: Yes. It covers workspace files, filters, shared lockfiles, catalogs, overrides, patches, and recursive commands.

faq.question: Can it read private registry tokens?
faq.answer: It advises checking .npmrc configuration. Users should avoid exposing token values in prompts, logs, or generated output.

faq.question: Is this limited to pnpm 10?
faq.answer: The skill states it is based on pnpm 10.x. Verify behavior against your installed pnpm version for critical workflows.

faq.question: Is it safe for production repositories?
faq.answer: It is usable with caution. Review commands before execution, especially install, publish, patch, removal, and migration commands.
